did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and validated a deep learning-based image classification method for the pathological diagnosis of Extramammary Paget's Disease (EMPD), a rare cutaneous malignancy. Specifically, it used five deep convolutional neural networks (ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, and VGG16) via transfer learning to distinguish between Paget's cells and normal cells in H&E-stained whole-slide images. The study achieved an accuracy of 95.522% at ×40 magnification using ResNet34.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. The study involves the development and validation of a deep learning-based image classification method for Extramammary Paget's Disease using five specific neural networks (ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, and VGG16) on a dataset of 584 whole-slide images. It presents original experimental results, including accuracy metrics (95.522% for ResNet34), and describes the methodology, dataset construction, and evaluation indices, which are characteristic of an original research article rather than a review.

title: Development and Validation of an Artificial Intelligence-Based Image Classification Method for Pathological Diagnosis in Patients With Extramammary Paget’s Disease
brief_summary: This study developed and validated a deep learning-based image classification method for the pathological diagnosis of Extramammary Paget’s Disease (EMPD), a rare cutaneous malignancy. The researchers utilized five deep convolutional neural networks (ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, and VGG16) via transfer learning to distinguish between Paget’s cells and normal cells in H&E-stained whole-slide images. The dataset consisted of 584 whole-slide images collected from the Dermatological Pathology Department of Huashan Hospital, Fudan University. The models were evaluated using accuracy, AUC-score, precision, recall, and other metrics on images at ×20 and ×40 magnifications. The ResNet34 model achieved the best performance with an accuracy of 95.522% at ×40 magnification. The results demonstrate that this AI method is quantitative, fast, and repeatable, potentially empowering grassroots pathologists to improve diagnostic efficiency and accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Distinguishing between Paget's cells (Extramammary Paget's Disease) and normal cells in H&E-stained whole-slide images.
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task. The study developed and validated deep learning models (ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, VGG16) to classify pathological images into two discrete categories: 'deviancy' (EMPD/Paget's cells) and 'normal' skin. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC-score, precision, recall, F1-score, sensitivity, and specificity. The best model (ResNet34) achieved an accuracy of 95.522% at ×40 magnification.

datasets_used.name: Huashan Hospital EMPD Dataset
datasets_used.description: A retrospective single-center dataset of H&E-stained whole-slide skin histopathology images collected from the Dermatological Pathology Department of Huashan Hospital, Fudan University, Shanghai, China. The dataset includes images scanned at ×20 and ×40 magnifications (1,920 × 1,088 pixels) using a NanoZoomer 2.0-RS system. Images were manually cropped by experienced dermatology pathologists.

use.training: Used to train five deep convolutional neural networks (ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, and VGG16) via transfer learning to distinguish between Paget's cells and normal cells.
use.validation: Used for model evaluation and validation of the classification performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the models' accuracy, AUC-score, precision, recall, F1-score, sensitivity, and specificity at different magnifications.
use.other: Used to build a single-center database to overcome morphological diversity challenges in histopathological images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, AUC-score, precision, recall, F1-score, sensitivity, and specificity, along with confusion matrices and ROC curves, without statistical significance testing between models.

Huashan Hospital EMPD Dataset (Retrospec.dataset_description: 584 H&E-stained whole-slide skin histopathology images (341 abnormal/Paget's cells, 243 normal) from Huashan Hospital, Fudan University. Images scanned at x20 and x40 magnifications.
Huashan Hospital EMPD Dataset (Retrospec.evaluation_setting: Binary classification of Paget's cells vs. normal cells using five deep convolutional neural networks via transfer learning. Evaluated on a test set of 100 skin pathological images.

ResNet34.notes: Best performance overall. At x40 magnification, accuracy was 0.9552 and AUC was 0.9524. At x20 magnification, accuracy was 0.9355 and AUC was 0.9524. In the 100-image test set, accuracy was 0.9500 and AUC was 0.9511. Correctly distinguished 95 out of 100 images.

ResNet50.notes: Second best performance. ROC curve was second closest to the upper left corner.

VGG16.notes: Took the longest prediction time (24.5982s for 100 images).

GoogLeNet.notes: Lowest prediction accuracy and lowest AUC-score among the models. Loss decreased rapidly then changed slowly.

MobileNetV2.notes: Took the least prediction time (11.9645s for 100 images) due to lightweight structure. Loss decreased slowly with small fluctuations.

ResNet34.notes: Highest precision, recall, F1-score, sensitivity, and specificity for both classes compared to other models.

magnification_comparison.notes: Accuracy was slightly higher at x40 than x20, but the difference in AUC-score was not significant.

Huashan Hospital EMPD Dataset (Retrospec.notes: The ResNet34 model achieved the best performance. The study demonstrated that the AI method is quantitative, fast, and repeatable. Class Activation Maps (CAM) were used to visualize regions of interest, showing the network paid more attention to lesion areas for nested Paget's cells.

architecture_used.main_model: Five deep convolutional neural networks: ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, and VGG16
architecture_used.pretraining: Initial weights inherited from pretrained models trained on ImageNet (except for the fully connected layer)
architecture_used.input_size: 1,920 × 1,088 pixels (cropped from whole-slide images scanned at ×20 and ×40 magnifications)

ResNet34.structure: Uses basic blocks composed of two 3 × 3 convolutions

ResNet50.structure: Uses bottleneck modules composed of 1 × 1, 3 × 3, and 1 × 1 convolutions

VGG16.structure: 16 weight layers, uses 3 × 3 convolution kernels, three fully connected layers, ReLU activation

GoogLeNet.structure: Inception structure, uses 1 × 1 convolution for dimensionality reduction, auxiliary classifiers, no fully connected layer

MobileNetV2.structure: Depth-wise and point-wise convolutions, inverted residuals, linear bottleneck, no ReLU after last point-wise convolution

training_details.data_split: 6:2:2 ratio for training (375 images), validation (109 images), and testing (100 images)

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer modified for specific classification task

preprocessing_techniques: Manual cropping of whole-slide images by experienced dermatology pathologists
Random cropping of collected skin pathology images into different sizes and aspect ratios
Scaling cropped images to a fixed size of 224 × 224 pixels
Random horizontal flipping of images with a probability of 0.5
Pixel normalization processing (dividing pixel values by 255 to convert to range [0, 1])

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Collected skin pathology images were randomly cropped into different sizes and aspect ratios.
Cropped images were scaled to a fixed size of 224 × 224 pixels.
Images were randomly flipped horizontally with a probability of 0.5.
Pixel values were divided by 255 to convert them to the range [0, 1].

conclusions: The study successfully developed and validated a deep learning-based image classification method using five convolutional neural networks (ResNet34, ResNet50, MobileNetV2, GoogLeNet, and VGG16) to distinguish between Paget’s cells and normal cells in Extramammary Paget’s Disease (EMPD) pathological images.

ResNet34 achieved the best overall performance, with an accuracy of 95.522% and an AUC-score of 0.9524 at ×40 magnification, and an accuracy of 95.00% and AUC-score of 0.9511 on the test set of 100 images.
The proposed AI method is quantitative, fast, and repeatable, demonstrating potential to empower grassroots pathologists to improve diagnostic efficiency and accuracy for EMPD.
MobileNetV2 required the least prediction time (11.9645s for 100 images) due to its lightweight structure, while VGG16 required the longest time (24.5982s) due to its deeper network architecture.
GoogLeNet exhibited the lowest prediction accuracy and AUC-score among the tested models.

The study utilized a single-center retrospective dataset of 584 whole-slide images (341 abnormal, 243 normal) from Huashan Hospital, Fudan University, noting that this data-driven approach helps overcome morphological diversity challenges in histopathological images.

The research addresses a gap in AI applications for dermatology by focusing on EMPD, a rare cutaneous malignancy often ignored by algorithms designed for common skin cancers like melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma.
important_remarks: The study is limited by its retrospective, single-center design using data exclusively from Huashan Hospital, Fudan University, which introduces potential selection bias and limits generalizability to other populations or institutions.

The dataset consists of 584 whole-slide images from an Asian population; the authors acknowledge that AI diagnostic systems may perform differently on different skin types and races, suggesting a need for multicenter prospective validation with diverse ethnicities.

The study addresses a rare disease (Extramammary Paget's Disease) with low incidence, which contributes to the small sample size and the challenge of morphological diversity (e.g., atypical cells, scattered distribution, inflammatory cell infiltration) that can lead to misdiagnosis.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to compare model performances; conclusions about the superiority of ResNet34 are based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC) rather than statistical significance tests.

The dataset includes images from 286 EMPD patients and 243 normal skin images, but the specific patient-level split for training, validation, and testing is not explicitly detailed beyond the image counts (375 training, 109 validation, 100 testing), raising potential concerns about data leakage if images from the same patient appear in multiple sets.
The models were trained using transfer learning with ImageNet weights, which may not fully capture histopathological features specific to skin tissue, although this is a common practice.

The study highlights that early-stage EMPD cells may present with atypical morphology and be mixed with inflammatory cells, making them difficult to distinguish, which is a known clinical challenge that the AI model attempts to address but may still struggle with in ambiguous cases.
